content:
# Matthew Chapter 1 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE, Quire A, first folio of Matthew | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_001_page_001.jpg, MAT_001_page_002.jpg

1. [The] book of [the] genesis of Yeshua Christos, son of David, son of Abraam.

2. Abraam begat [the] Isaak, and Isaak begat [the] Iakob, and Iakob begat [the] Ioudas and the brothers of him.

3. And Ioudas begat [the] Phares and [the] Zara out of the Thamar, and Phares begat [the] Esrom, and Esrom begat [the] Aram.

4. And Aram begat [the] Aminadab, and Aminadab begat [the] Naasson, and Naasson begat [the] Salmon.

5. And Salmon begat [the] Boes out of the Rachab, and Boes begat [the] Iobed out of the Routh, and Iobed begat [the] Iessai.

6. And Iessai begat [the] David the king. And David begat [the] Solomon out of the [wife] of the Ourias.

7. And Solomon begat [the] Roboam, and Roboam begat [the] Abia, and Abia begat [the] Asaph.

8. And Asaph begat [the] Iosaphat, and Iosaphat begat [the] Ioram, and Ioram begat [the] Ozias.

9. And Ozias begat [the] Ioatham, and Ioatham begat [the] Achaz, and Achaz begat [the] Ezekias.

10. And Ezekias begat [the] Manasses, and Manasses begat [the] Amos, and Amos begat [the] Iosias.

11. And Iosias begat [the] Iechonias and the brothers of him upon the deportation of Babylon.

12. And after the deportation of Babylon, Iechonias begat [the] Salathiel, and Salathiel begat [the] Zorobabel.

13. And Zorobabel begat [the] Abioud, and Abioud begat [the] Eliakim, and Eliakim begat [the] Azor.

14. And Azor begat [the] Sadok, and Sadok begat [the] Achim, and Achim begat [the] Elioud.

15. And Elioud begat [the] Eleazar, and Eleazar begat [the] Matthan, and Matthan begat [the] Iakob.

16. And Iakob begat [the] Ioseph the husband of Mariam, out of whom was begotten Yeshua, the [one] being called Christos.

17. All the generations, therefore, from Abraam until David, generations fourteen; and from David until the deportation of Babylon, generations fourteen; and from the deportation of Babylon until the Christos, generations fourteen.

18. Now of the Yeshua Christos the genesis was thus: the mother of him, Mariam, having been betrothed to the Ioseph, before the coming together of them, she was found having [child] in [the] womb out of [the] Spirit Holy.

19. And Ioseph the husband of her, being righteous and not willing to make-a-public-example of her, purposed to release her secretly.

20. And these [things] of him having considered, behold, an angel of [the] Lord according to a dream appeared to him, saying: "Ioseph, son of David, do not fear to take Mariam the wife of you, for the [thing] in her having been begotten out of [the] Spirit is Holy.

21. And she will bear a son, and you shall call the name of him Yeshua, for he will save the people of him from the sins of them."

22. And this whole [thing] has come to pass in order that might be fulfilled the [word] having been spoken by [the] Lord through the prophet, saying:

23. "Behold, the virgin in [the] womb will have and will bear a son, and they will call the name of him Emmanouel," which is, being translated, "With us [is] the God."

24. And having been raised up, the Ioseph from the sleep, he did as the angel of [the] Lord commanded him, and he took the wife of him.

25. And he was not knowing her until which [time] she bore a son, and he called the name of him Yeshua.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Βίβλος γενέσεως (v.1):** Literally "book of genesis/origin/generation." The word γένεσις carries the double meaning of origin-story and genealogical record. I have preserved the transliteration "genesis" to maintain this ambiguity. The Sinaiticus text reads ΒΙΒΛΟΣ ΓΕΝΕΣΕΩΣ clearly in the first column.

2. **Name forms:** All proper names are transliterated directly from the Greek forms in Sinaiticus rather than normalized to Hebrew or English conventional forms. Thus: Abraam (not Abraham), Iakob (not Jacob), Ioudas (not Judah), Mariam (not Mary). The name Yeshua is used for Ἰησοῦς to preserve the Hebrew etymology referenced in v.21 ("he will save").

3. **Ἀσάφ (v.7-8):** Sinaiticus reads Ασαφ (Asaph) where the Masoretic genealogy and most later manuscripts read Ασα (Asa). This is a notable Sinaiticus variant — Asaph is the name of a psalmist (Psalms 50, 73-83), not the king of Judah. The reading is shared with Vaticanus.

4. **Ἀμώς (v.10):** Sinaiticus reads Αμως (Amos) where later manuscripts read Αμων (Amon). Again, this appears to be the name of the prophet rather than the king. Another Sinaiticus-Vaticanus agreement against the Byzantine text.

5. **ἐγεννήθη (v.16):** The passive voice "was begotten" (ἐγεννήθη) in v.16 is significant — throughout the genealogy the active ἐγέννησεν ("begat") is used. The shift to passive at v.16 breaks the pattern precisely at the point where the text avoids saying Joseph begat Jesus. The Sinaiticus is clear on this reading.

6. **δειγματίσαι (v.19):** "To make a public example" — this is the word used in Sinaiticus. Some manuscripts read παραδειγματίσαι (a strengthened form). The Sinaiticus reading is the simpler form.

7. **ἐν γαστρὶ ἕξει (v.23):** Literally "in womb will have" — the future tense of the Isaiah 7:14 quotation. The Hebrew original uses עַלְמָה (almah, "young woman"), but Matthew's Greek quotes the LXX which has παρθένος (parthenos, "virgin").

8. **οὐκ ἐγίνωσκεν (v.25):** The imperfect tense "was not knowing" — I have preserved the continuous aspect. The verb γινώσκω in this context carries the Hebrew sense of intimate/sexual knowledge.

9. **Textual variant at v.25:** Sinaiticus reads "a son" (υἱόν) where some later manuscripts add "her firstborn son" (τὸν υἱὸν αὐτῆς τὸν πρωτότοκον). The shorter Sinaiticus reading is preserved here.

— EMET, AI Translator
